rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing reliably achieves uniformity for most products, with rare issues for sensitive formulations. Adequate pre-fill mixing takes defined time that is scheduled. Mixing is largely automated per validated recipes, low ongoing skill. Mixing energy is a trivial cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dissolved oxygen outside the product-specific range during hold
rows.imp_rat: Dissolved oxygen out of range affects oxidative product stability; relevant to condition control with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Nitrogen overlay reliably holds DO for products that need it, though DO monitoring is not always continuous. DO changes gradually so response time is usually adequate. Overlay gas control is largely automated, low skill demand. Inert gas overlay is a modest utility cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of vessel-pressure deviation from allowable limits during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Vessel-pressure deviation during transfer can stress the boundary; relevant with moderate-serious consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records containing inaccurate or missing observations and response actions

rows.sat_rat: Deviation records frequently contain gaps or inaccuracies in observations and actions. Deviation documentation is done post-event, adding effort. Complete, accurate deviation records require trained diligence. Inaccurate deviation records prolong investigations, cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between readiness records and the vessel state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between readiness records and vessel state is a records-fidelity ambiguity, peripheral to the containment job (low relevance); usually correctable before transfer (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual record-keeping leaves recurring discrepancies between records and actual state. Reconciling records to vessel state is a review-time task. Spotting record-vs-state discrepancies needs attentive review. Low cost beyond labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt when holding allowance and fill-finish readiness indications conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt when holding allowance and readiness indications conflict is a scheduling ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); resolvable coordination matter (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of bulk receipt beyond the approved transfer window
rows.imp_rat: Receipt extending beyond the approved transfer window is a scheduling/timing delay, only loosely tied to containment (low relevance); typically an absorbable timing overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled transfer rates usually complete within window, though occasional flow issues extend it. The transfer window duration is the explicit time metric here. Transfer rate is largely equipment-set, needing little skill. Overlong transfers tie up equipment time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of bulk product lost through leakage or spillage during receipt
rows.imp_rat: Leakage or spillage during receipt is a yield-loss/waste concern, marginal to aseptic containment integrity itself (low relevance); economic loss rather than patient harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed connections keep leakage/spillage low, with occasional connection losses remaining. Leakage occurs during the transfer window, not as time loss. Proper connections limit spillage with modest skill. Product loss through leakage is a direct material cost the setup aims to minimize.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of additional vessel holding caused by downstream unavailability after release eligibility
rows.imp_rat: Extra holding from downstream unavailability after eligibility is a scheduling delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable timing overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling coordination limits it, but downstream bottlenecks still force additional holding with some regularity. Extra holding from downstream unavailability is a pure waiting-time loss. This depends on scheduling coordination rather than operator skill. Extended holding consumes utilities and risks approaching hold limits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of releasable bulk product remaining in the vessel after discharge
rows.imp_rat: Releasable bulk remaining after discharge is a yield/waste issue, peripheral to aseptic containment (low relevance); economic loss without safety consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vessel geometry and drain design recover most product reliably, though some heel is routinely left behind. Residual recovery adds minor time at discharge end. Draining technique is straightforward. Product left in the vessel is direct yield loss, the central concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required pre-hold results or approvals after the scheduled start
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for pre-hold results/approvals after scheduled start is a process-timing delay, marginal to the hold job (low relevance); mostly a schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows are established but result and sign-off delays routinely push start beyond schedule. Waiting for pre-hold results or approvals is a direct start-delay time loss. Little operator skill affects the wait; it depends on lab and QA turnaround. Idle equipment and staff during waits carry opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about aseptic decision authority when directions conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about aseptic decision authority when directions conflict is a governance ambiguity, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); resolvable through escalation (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting holding deadlines governing the same vessel batch
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting holding deadlines for the same batch is a governance ambiguity, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); resolvable through clarification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting holding deadlines occasionally arise from overlapping validated limits and manual tracking. Reconciling deadlines adds minor scheduling time. Managing multiple hold limits needs planning skill. Scheduling systems add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of remaining holding margin consumed by unplanned vessel retention
rows.imp_rat: Holding margin consumed by unplanned retention is a schedule-efficiency delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Retention overruns still occur from unplanned events, eroding margin. Unplanned retention extends holding time and defers throughput. Reducing unplanned retention is a scheduling discipline issue, not a skill gap. Consumed margin from unplanned retention wastes validated hold capacity and risks batch value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of related contamination signals not connected to the held-solution deviation
rows.imp_rat: Contamination signals not connected to the held-solution deviation is an investigation-linkage defect, tangential to physically maintaining containment (low relevance); a detection-completeness gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Related signals are often not connected to a deviation because systems are siloed. Connecting related signals takes investigative time. Recognizing related contamination signals requires broad situational awareness. Trending tools add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the acceptance criteria applied to the held solution's evidence
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for held-solution evidence is an interpretive documentation matter, distant from actual containment (low relevance); resolvable through clarification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility acceptance criteria are relatively well defined, leaving limited but real ambiguity. Ambiguity forces clarifying cycles that cost time. Interpreting ambiguous acceptance criteria falls to expert judgement. Clarification meetings consume resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from completion of required monitoring to evidence availability for sterility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Time from monitoring completion to evidence availability is a data-latency delay, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable timing gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated data flow makes monitoring results promptly available, though manual entry can add lag. Once monitoring completes, electronic systems make results available for assessment quickly. Data transfer is automated, needing little skill. LIMS/historian integration is an established cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical holding records with unverifiable provenance or completeness
rows.imp_rat: Holding records with unverifiable provenance is a documentation-integrity defect, peripheral to actually containing the solution (low relevance); a traceability gap rather than product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails and controlled records make unverifiable provenance of critical records fairly uncommon where electronic. Verifying provenance adds review time. Provenance checks are proceduralized needing modest skill. Data-integrity controls add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among approved disposition criteria applied by reviewing parties
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among reviewers' disposition criteria is a governance harmonization issue, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); consequences are reconcilable procedural variation (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Written acceptance criteria reduce but do not eliminate inconsistency across reviewers. Aligning reviewers' criteria can add discussion time. Consistent application of criteria depends on reviewer training and calibration. Governance and training add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-finish interruption while sterile bulk remains in the vessel
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish interruption while bulk remains in the vessel is a downstream schedule delay, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Fill-finish interruptions occur intermittently from line issues, affecting hold outcomes. Fill-finish interruptions while bulk waits directly prolong idle vessel time. Reducing interruptions is an operations coordination matter, not skill. Interruptions waste line capacity and hold margin.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validated holding margin consumed by quality disposition delay
rows.imp_rat: Validated holding margin consumed by disposition delay is a schedule-efficiency issue, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Disposition delays routinely eat into margin because of slow sterility confirmation. Disposition delay, driven by test turnaround, consumes hold margin. Speeding disposition is limited by test timelines, not staff skill. Margin consumed by QA delay is lost validated hold value and risks batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validated holding margin consumed by upstream staging before vessel receipt
rows.imp_rat: Holding margin consumed by upstream staging before receipt is a schedule-efficiency waste, peripheral to containment (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Staging overruns intermittently reduce margin depending on upstream timing. Upstream staging before receipt shortens available hold window. Managing staging is a coordination task, not skill-limited. Margin consumed by upstream staging is wasted validated capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disagreement among quality, manufacturing, and technical parties over deviation impact
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among parties over deviation impact is a coordination delay, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); resolvable through review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Escalation forums help but disagreements among QA, manufacturing and technical parties persist and remain time-consuming. Cross-functional disagreement over deviation impact can drag on and directly delay disposition. Resolution depends on senior alignment and negotiation skill across functions. Prolonged disagreement consumes expert time and delays valuable throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among KPI records used for the same held bulk
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among KPI records for the same bulk is a data-reconciliation ambiguity, tangential to containment (low relevance); correctable record discrepancy (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data systems reduce inconsistency but multiple record sources still produce recurring mismatches for the same batch. Reconciling inconsistent KPI records adds time before a defensible disposition. Spotting inconsistencies across records requires careful reviewer attention. Reconciliation is labor cost, generally modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in disposition criteria for borderline KPI results
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in disposition criteria for borderline KPI results is an interpretive edge-case matter, tangential to containment (low relevance); addressable by criteria refinement (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Criteria cover clear cases well but borderline KPI ambiguity is a recurring, poorly resolved issue. Ambiguity in borderline KPI criteria stalls decisions while interpretation is sought. Borderline calls fall back on expert judgement because criteria are underspecified. Ambiguous criteria risk inconsistent, costly decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejecting conforming held bulk because disposition evidence is misinterpreted
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting conforming bulk due to misinterpreted evidence is a waste/false-reject issue, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); economic loss without safety harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Review and second-checks reduce but do not eliminate misinterpretation leading to false rejection. Re-evaluating misinterpreted evidence to avoid wrong rejection adds review cycles. Correct interpretation of disposition evidence depends on experienced reviewer judgement. Rejecting conforming bulk needlessly destroys valuable product, a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from completion of required quality evaluations to the bulk disposition decision
rows.imp_rat: Time from quality evaluations to disposition decision is a process-latency delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Defined disposition workflows shorten this interval, though queueing and approvals still add recurring delay. The gap from completed evaluations to the disposition decision reflects review queueing that varies. Prompt decisions depend on availability of authorized decision-makers. Decision delay idles downstream capacity, a schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the bulk solution reaches the sterile bulk hold vessel after its scheduled arrival
rows.imp_rat: Bulk reaching the vessel after scheduled arrival is a timing/delay issue, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Production scheduling makes on-time arrival usual, but upstream slippage still causes recurring late arrivals. Late bulk arrival directly delays the hold and downstream fill-finish schedule. Timely arrival depends on upstream coordination rather than overseer skill. Late arrival can compress hold windows and idle capacity, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time that the sterile bulk hold vessel remains unavailable beyond the scheduled bulk arrival
rows.imp_rat: Vessel remaining unavailable beyond scheduled arrival is a readiness/timing delay, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); a schedule impact only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vessel scheduling and turnaround planning usually ensure availability, with occasional recurring conflicts. Vessel unavailability beyond scheduled arrival forces waiting that delays the hold. Vessel turnaround/cleaning is a routine, moderately skilled operation. Idle bulk waiting for a vessel risks schedule and material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the approved bulk-hold schedule and fill-finish handoff schedule
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between bulk-hold and fill-finish handoff schedules is a scheduling-coordination ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); an addressable timing misalignment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling tools help but hold-vs-handoff misalignment recurs given cross-unit dependencies. Mismatch between hold and handoff schedules directly creates waiting or rushing at the interface. Aligning schedules requires planning coordination skill across units. Schedule mismatch idles capacity or risks hold-limit breach, a cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the fill-finish receiving interface is unavailable at the scheduled handoff
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish receiving interface unavailable at handoff is a downstream-readiness delay, tangential to containment (low relevance); a schedule/coordination impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Handoff coordination is generally reliable, though occasional interface unavailability still disrupts the scheduled transfer. An unavailable receiving interface at handoff delays transfer and can consume hold time. Interface readiness is a coordination task rather than a deep-skill dependency. Handoff delay idles both units, a scheduling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of criterion revision caused by late discovery of sterile bulk process constraints
rows.imp_rat: Criterion revision from late discovery of process constraints is a rework/planning issue, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stage-gate reviews reduce late surprises but late constraint discovery still recurs. Late discovery of process constraints forces criterion rework, and the primary loss is the schedule churn this causes. Early constraint capture depends on experienced process input that is not always available up front. Rework consumes engineering resources but the felt loss is schedule slip.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical specification claims lacking defensible technical evidence
rows.imp_rat: Specification claims lacking defensible evidence is a documentation-support ambiguity, tangential to containment (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evidence review is standard but critical claims still sometimes rest on thin data. Securing defensible evidence for each critical claim extends timelines. Assessing evidentiary defensibility requires seasoned technical reviewers. Weakly supported claims trigger costly additional studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable quality and regulatory expectations omitted from the recommendation rationale
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory expectations omitted from the recommendation rationale is a documentation-completeness defect, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); an addressable narrative gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory rationale is standard in submissions, and coverage is generally solid though occasional gaps appear. Compiling the full set of applicable expectations into the rationale takes effort but delay is secondary. Comprehensive regulatory mapping depends on QA/RA specialists whose availability limits completeness. Omitted expectations trigger audit findings with costly remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specification requirements lacking traceability to supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Specification requirements lacking evidence traceability is a documentation-support ambiguity, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are common practice yet completeness varies, so untraced requirements recur. Tracing every spec requirement to evidence adds documentation time. Maintaining traceability matrices demands disciplined validation practice. Untraceable requirements cause rework and audit cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among evidence sources supporting the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contradictions among evidence sources for the recommendation is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, distant from containment (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Review processes catch many contradictions but some persist into the recommendation. Resolving evidence contradictions adds investigation time. Adjudicating conflicting sources requires senior technical judgement. Unresolved contradictions cause rework and delayed decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supporting evidence with unverified origin, currency, or integrity
rows.imp_rat: Supporting evidence with unverified origin/currency/integrity is a documentation-provenance defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity practices (ALCOA+) are established, so most evidence provenance is verifiable, though gaps remain. Verifying provenance and currency of evidence takes administrative time. Assessing data integrity requires data-governance literacy. Unverified evidence forces re-sourcing at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in applying evaluation criteria across sterile bulk containment options
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency applying evaluation criteria across containment options is a governance-consistency issue, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); a review-process variation (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured comparison methods exist but consistency across options still varies by evaluator. Inconsistent application forces re-scoring loops that waste time secondarily. Uniform application across options depends on disciplined, experienced evaluators, the core skill gap here. Inconsistency leads to re-work and disputed decisions with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material safety, quality, capacity, and lifecycle tradeoffs unexplained in the recommendation rationale
rows.imp_rat: Tradeoffs unexplained in the recommendation rationale is a documentation-completeness ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); a narrative gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Tradeoff documentation exists but depth is inconsistent, leaving decision rationale partially unsupported. Explaining tradeoffs thoroughly lengthens rationale writing but time is not the core loss. Articulating safety, quality, capacity and lifecycle tradeoffs coherently demands broad multidisciplinary judgement that is scarce. Unexplained tradeoffs lead to poor selections carrying lifecycle cost penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-loss controls omitted from the containment recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Product-loss controls omitted from the recommendation is a completeness gap tied to yield, peripheral to aseptic containment (low relevance); economic-loss oriented (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Loss-control practices exist but are inconsistently embedded in containment recommendations. Adding product-loss controls to the recommendation adds design effort. Identifying appropriate loss controls needs process-design expertise. Omitted loss controls directly waste expensive bulk product, a clear cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch volumes lacking representative fit evidence
rows.imp_rat: Batch volumes lacking representative fit evidence is a documentation-support ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); a validation-evidence gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scale/fit assessment is routine but representative evidence across all required volumes is often incomplete. Generating representative fit evidence across batch volumes takes study time. Designing volume-representative studies needs process-engineering competence. Lacking fit evidence risks costly rescoping of vessel sizing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic compatibility evidence not traceable to the evaluated configuration and hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility evidence not traceable to the evaluated configuration is a documentation-traceability ambiguity, distant from actual holding (low relevance); a defensibility gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability discipline is improving but evidence-to-configuration mismatch remains a common weakness. Establishing traceability of compatibility evidence to the exact configuration is time-consuming. Judging whether evidence truly matches the evaluated config/hold conditions requires validation expertise. Non-traceable evidence forces repeat studies, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between evaluation criteria and applicable quality and regulatory expectations
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between evaluation criteria and regulatory expectations is a compliance-alignment documentation issue, distant from the hold job (low relevance); reconcilable before finalization (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory review is a routine gate, so major conflicts are usually caught, though subtle ones persist. Cross-checking criteria against the regulatory landscape adds review time but is not a delay-dominated need. Reconciling criteria with evolving GMP/Annex requires specialized regulatory knowledge in short supply. Conflicts caught late in inspection or audit are costly to remediate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria permitting inconsistent evaluator interpretation
rows.imp_rat: Criteria permitting inconsistent evaluator interpretation is a wording ambiguity, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); addressable by clearer criteria (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Rubric-based scoring exists but wording rigor varies, so inconsistent interpretation remains a recurring gap. Ambiguous criteria cause rework loops that cost time, but the root gain is reducing interpretation dependence. When criteria permit varied evaluator readings, correct scoring hinges on individual judgement, exactly the skill dependence this need targets. Inconsistent interpretation causes re-evaluation effort, a modest resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria lacking defined evidence for claim verification
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria lacking defined verification evidence is a methodological documentation gap, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); correctable review artifact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Requirements-traceability practices exist but are unevenly applied to evaluation criteria, leaving verification gaps. Defining verification evidence per criterion upfront slows criteria finalization somewhat. Knowing what constitutes defensible evidence for each claim requires validation and regulatory literacy that is not universal. Undefined evidence needs cause redundant testing later, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved criterion conflicts among quality, manufacturing, engineering, and validation stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved criterion conflicts among stakeholders is a governance-alignment ambiguity, distant from the hold job (low relevance); resolvable through coordination (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Governance forums exist but genuine criterion alignment is often incomplete at decision time. Reconciling stakeholder criterion conflicts drags out decision timelines. Facilitating alignment among quality, manufacturing, engineering and validation requires strong cross-functional leadership, the scarce capability here. Unresolved conflicts cause rework and suboptimal decisions with indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions across supplier, facility, and quality information about vessel containment
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among supplier/facility/quality info about containment is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, peripheral to the physical hold (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved contradictions across information sources are a common friction point. Resolving contradictions across sources takes coordination time. Reconciling supplier/facility/quality data needs cross-functional expertise. Coordination is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containment conclusions lacking traceable links to assessment criteria, assumptions, and supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Containment conclusions lacking traceable links to evidence is a documentation-traceability defect, peripheral to the physical hold (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions sometimes lack full traceable links to underlying assumptions and evidence. Building traceability adds documentation time. Linking conclusions to criteria and evidence needs rigorous documentation discipline. Traceability effort is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting acceptance-criterion definitions across qualification documents
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance-criterion definitions across qualification documents is a documentation-consistency ambiguity, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); reconcilable editorial matter (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting criterion definitions across documents still occur despite review. Reconciling definitions across documents adds review time. Harmonizing criterion definitions needs careful editorial control. Reconciliation is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required protocol evidence lacking traceability to challenge conditions and acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Protocol evidence lacking traceability to challenge conditions is a documentation-traceability defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protocol evidence sometimes lacks traceability to conditions and criteria. Cross-referencing evidence is documentation effort. Mapping evidence to challenge conditions and criteria needs meticulous discipline. Documentation labor is the main cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of foreseeable deviations and invalid-run conditions lacking predefined handling in the protocol
rows.imp_rat: Foreseeable deviations lacking predefined handling in the protocol is a study-design rework gap, tangential to containment (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Foreseeable deviations often lack predefined handling, causing ad hoc decisions. Predefining deviation handling is upfront drafting, saving time later. Anticipating deviations and invalid-run conditions requires experienced foresight. Predefinition is low-cost preparation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification evidence lacking complete source-data traceability
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence lacking source-data traceability is a documentation-integrity defect, peripheral to actual containment (low relevance); affects data defensibility not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature data-integrity expectations mean source traceability is usually reasonably complete. Source-data traceability is a records task. Complete data lineage requires disciplined data-management practice. Data-integrity systems carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protocol deviations lacking documented resolution before study disposition
rows.imp_rat: Protocol deviations lacking documented resolution before disposition is a study-closure rework gap, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation-management systems make undocumented resolutions relatively rare. Resolving deviations before disposition can delay closeout. Documented deviation resolution relies on QA competence that is well-established. Investigation and resolution consume labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required containment evidence lacking traceability to evaluated operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Containment evidence lacking traceability to operating conditions is a documentation-traceability defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); affects defensibility not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Some evidence lacks clear traceability to the conditions it represents. Evidence linkage is documentation work, not a bottleneck. Tying evidence to operating conditions requires careful record management. Traceability is a labor overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of detected extractable compounds without defensible chemical identities
rows.imp_rat: Extractable compounds without defensible identities is an analytical-characterization ambiguity, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); affects data defensibility not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Many detected extractables remain unidentified, so defensible identity is often incomplete, leaving this poorly served. Resolving chemical identities of extractables is time-consuming and iterative. Assigning defensible structures to unknown peaks requires scarce expert spectroscopists. Advanced identification (high-res MS/NMR) is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of background contaminants falsely attributed to vessel contact materials
rows.imp_rat: Background contaminants falsely attributed to contact materials is an attribution/analytical issue, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); affects investigation accuracy not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: False attribution of background contaminants to the vessel persists despite standard blank practices, leaving a moderate gap. Ruling out background contaminants adds analytical iteration. Correct attribution requires skilled use of blanks and controls. Additional control runs add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of extractable compounds lost or transformed during specimen handling and preparation
rows.imp_rat: Loss/transformation of extractables during specimen handling is a lab-method fidelity defect, distant from containment (low relevance); impacts characterization data quality only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Extractable loss or transformation during specimen prep is a recognized, only partially mitigated error source. Minimizing prep losses requires careful, sometimes slower handling. Avoiding loss or transformation of extractables during prep demands skilled analysts. Careful prep adds modest labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in measured extractables profiles across equivalent specimens and characterization runs
rows.imp_rat: Variation in extractables profiles across specimens is an analytical-reproducibility concern, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); affects characterization confidence only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Extractables profile variability across runs is a recognized issue but managed acceptably by standardized protocols. Repeat characterization runs add time. Reducing extractables profile variability depends on analytical rigor typically available. Repeat characterization adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reported extractables results lacking traceability to specimens, exposure conditions, or raw observations
rows.imp_rat: Extractables results lacking traceability to specimens/conditions is an analytical-documentation ambiguity, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); a data-defensibility gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity requirements and LIMS make extractables result traceability fairly reliable, so this is well served. Documenting result traceability is routine within reporting. Maintaining specimen-to-result traceability is a standard LIMS-supported skill. LIMS and documentation add ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of unresolved conflict among toxicological sources used to evaluate identified leachables
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among toxicological sources for leachables is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); addressable in assessment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved conflicts among tox sources persist because data are heterogeneous, leaving conclusions uncertain. Reconciling conflicting tox sources adds analysis time. Adjudicating among conflicting toxicological data requires senior toxicology judgement. Resolution effort is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of extraneous contamination misattributed to migration from vessel contact materials
rows.imp_rat: Extraneous contamination misattributed to material migration is an investigation-attribution issue, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); affects root-cause accuracy not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Background contamination is frequently misattributed despite blanks and controls, a recurring nuisance only partly controlled. rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of leachable alteration during sample storage, handling, and preparation
rows.imp_rat: Leachable alteration during sample storage/handling is an analytical-integrity concern, peripheral to the physical hold CFJ (low relevance); affects measurement accuracy not the hold itself (low severity).

Preventing leachable alteration needs trained sample-handling discipline. Stabilized handling and cold storage add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in leachable measurements across equivalent samples from the same hold condition
rows.imp_rat: Variation in leachable measurements across equivalent samples is an analytical-reproducibility issue, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); affects data confidence not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Method validation controls leachable measurement variability reasonably well for validated methods, so this is moderately served. Replicate leachable testing adds analytical time. Controlling inter-sample variation requires solid method-validation skill that is fairly standard. Replicate testing increases cost per condition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve the identities of leachable signals needed for the safety assessment
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve leachable signal identities is an analytical-workflow delay, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); a schedule impact on assessment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The prolonged identification cycle is a chronic time pain with no fast standard solution, so this is poorly served. Rapid identification via advanced instrumentation is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reported leachable results lacking traceable sample provenance or reviewable calculations
rows.imp_rat: Leachable results lacking traceable provenance is an analytical-documentation ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); affects data defensibility not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Leachable result provenance and reviewable calculations are reasonably well maintained under GMP data-integrity practice. Provenance documentation is embedded in reporting workflows. Capturing sample provenance and calculations is standard QC documentation skill. Documentation systems add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among product, process, and material-compatibility evidence used for the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among compatibility evidence for the recommendation is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, distant from actual containment (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting evidence is common and reconciliation is inconsistent, making the recommendation's basis only partially reliable. Reconciling multi-domain evidence adds review time. Integrating conflicting product/process/material evidence into a recommendation is a high-expertise synthesis task. Mostly labor cost for cross-functional review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of current product-contact components not traceable to material identities covered by suitability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Traceability of contact components to suitability evidence is a documentation-provenance ambiguity, peripheral to the physical hold job (low relevance); mostly a paperwork gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Gaps in linking current components to suitability-covered identities occur, especially with complex supply chains, leaving a real gap. rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of claimed economic benefit duplicated across site investment cases
rows.imp_rat: Benefit duplicated across site cases is a financial double-count; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-case duplication is poorly controlled where site cases are prepared in isolation. Cross-checking benefit against other site cases adds coordination time. rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among assumptions used to compare competing site investments
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent assumptions across site comparisons is analysis variability; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-site assumption consistency is rarely enforced, making comparisons unreliable. Aligning assumptions across competing site cases takes extra coordination time. Harmonizing cross-site assumptions requires portfolio-analysis expertise. Inconsistent cross-site assumptions distort capital allocation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital is committed before facility, utility, and fill-finish dependencies are ready
rows.imp_rat: Committing capital before dependencies ready is a timing-instability error; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Stage-gate and readiness reviews exist but dependency misalignment still occurs. Committing capital before dependencies are ready causes idle assets and rework delays. Sequencing capital against facility and fill-finish readiness requires program-coordination skill. Premature commitment strands capital before it can be productively deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of capital authorization delay beyond the date required by fill-finish plans
rows.imp_rat: Capital-authorization delay beyond the fill-finish date is a schedule error; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Gate timing is unpredictable because it depends on many contributors aligning. Authorization delays beyond the fill-finish-required date directly push out production timelines. Meeting the required date requires orchestration skill across multiple approvers. Delay can carry schedule-cost penalties but is primarily a timing failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production-demand or compliance changes make the approved capital priority obsolete before deployment
rows.imp_rat: Demand/compliance changes obsoleting capital priority is instability; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Priority obsolescence is an uncertain, poorly-hedged risk in status-quo planning. Demand or compliance shifts can obsolete a priority before deployment, wasting elapsed time. Anticipating demand and compliance change requires strategic forecasting expertise. Obsolete priorities strand committed capital on the wrong asset.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required site integration, qualification, and commissioning expenditure omitted from the capital outlay
rows.imp_rat: Omitting integration/qualification expenditure from capital outlay is a budgeting-completeness defect, remote from containment with financial consequence only.
rows.sat_rat: Omissions recur systematically because no standard checklist reliably captures the full integration scope. Integration and commissioning scoping is often deferred, so its cost appears only after the gate causing rework delays. Estimating site integration and CQV spend needs facilities and validation expertise the executor must coordinate across silos. Site integration, qualification and commissioning costs are among the most commonly omitted line items in capital outlays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the projected financial benefit from avoiding sterile bulk-solution loss
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in projected benefit from avoiding solution loss is a financial-estimation ambiguity, remote from containment with budgetary consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Uncertainty in avoided-loss benefit is high because contamination frequency data is sparse and inconsistent. Benefit projections are prepared at the gate but rest on assumptions that shift, causing rework. Estimating avoided bulk-solution loss requires probabilistic risk-modeling expertise rarely available. The projected financial benefit drives the justification but is speculative and often unsupported by hard data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that actual vessel utilization falls below the forecast used to calculate returns
rows.imp_rat: Utilization below forecast is a return-reliability error; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Actual utilization frequently falls short of optimistic gate forecasts. Utilization shortfalls only reveal themselves post-deployment, long after the gate. Forecasting realistic utilization requires demand-modeling expertise. Below-forecast utilization erodes the return that justified the capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of projected financial benefit attributable to factors outside the vessel investment
rows.imp_rat: Benefit attributable to non-vessel factors is a financial-attribution error; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Misattribution of benefit is a common and inconsistently corrected weakness. Isolating vessel-attributable benefit takes analytical time that delays justification. Attributing benefit strictly to the vessel investment requires disciplined causal analysis. Benefit from external factors inflates the vessel's apparent payback, misallocating capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recurring operation, maintenance, and requalification cost omitted from the return calculation
rows.imp_rat: Omitted recurring cost from the return calculation is a financial-estimation defect, remote from containment with budgetary consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Their omission recurs because return models emphasize benefits over sustaining costs. Requalification and maintenance costs are considered late if at all, delaying accurate return figures. Projecting requalification cadence and cost requires validation lifecycle expertise beyond the executor's reach. Recurring operation, maintenance and requalification costs are commonly left out of the return calculation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between capital outlay and realization of production benefits
rows.imp_rat: Delay between outlay and benefit realization is a financial-timing error; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Time-to-benefit is uncertain because ramp-up rarely follows plan exactly. The lag between outlay and benefit realization is a core payback-timing concern. Managing time-to-benefit requires deployment and ramp-up expertise. Delayed benefit realization erodes discounted returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected financial benefit duplicated across yield protection, batch-loss avoidance, and throughput
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated benefit across categories is a financial double-counting defect; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit duplication recurs because separate benefit categories overlap and are rarely reconciled. Untangling duplicated benefit streams adds analysis time before approval. Spotting double-counted benefits across yield, batch-loss and throughput needs modeling discipline. Duplicated benefit overstates return and risks approving marginal capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in projected returns caused by applying one bulk-solution value across economically different campaigns
rows.imp_rat: Single value across differing campaigns is a projection-error defect; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: A single blended value is commonly used, producing systematic projection error. Recomputing returns per campaign value adds modeling time. Segmenting bulk-solution value by campaign requires economic-modeling skill. Applying one value across different campaigns misstates returns and misallocates capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that projected financial returns fall below the capital authorization threshold
rows.imp_rat: Projected returns below the authorization threshold is a business-case error, remote from containment with funding consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Whether returns clear the threshold is uncertain because underlying benefit assumptions are shaky. Return shortfalls surface at the gate, sometimes forcing delays for rework. Building returns that clear the threshold requires financial-modeling skill applied to uncertain inputs. Returns below the authorization threshold jeopardize the capital and any sunk preparation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in total lifecycle cost at the capital approval gate
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle-cost uncertainty at the capital gate is a financial-estimation error, remote from aseptic containment with purely budgetary consequence.
rows.sat_rat: The certainty of the cost picture at the gate is low because inputs vary in quality and are rarely reconciled across contributors. Lifecycle cost figures are assembled at the gate but frequently arrive with unresolved assumptions that delay the decision. Building a credible total-lifecycle model requires specialist cost-engineering skill seldom resident in the approving function. Total lifecycle cost is routinely presented as a point estimate that understates operating and compliance costs, leaving wide uncertainty at approval.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which contamination, compliance, and production consequences of deferral remain unquantified
rows.imp_rat: Unquantified deferral consequences is an ambiguity/analysis gap; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Deferral consequences are routinely left unquantified, so the do-nothing baseline is unreliable. Quantifying deferral consequences adds analysis time when data is absent. Assessing contamination and compliance consequences of deferral requires risk expertise the executor lacks. Unquantified deferral consequences hide the cost of delay, distorting timing decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required authorization documents containing incomplete or invalid evidence

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among approvers on financial, quality, and production assumptions

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required finance, quality, engineering, and procurement concurrence

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier misses committed vessel delivery and qualification-support milestones

rows.sat_rat: Demand-to-fill-finish alignment is often approximate and uncertain. Aligning bulk-hold demand with fill-finish needs takes coordination time. Matching demand forecasts requires supply-planning expertise. Mismatch leads to over- or under-sized capital investment.

rows.imp_rat: Omitted production-disruption cost is a financial-implementation defect; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Its omission recurs because disruption is hard to forecast and often ignored. Production-disruption cost is assessed late, delaying complete implementation assumptions. rows.sat_rat: Assumption inconsistency across comparisons is common and inconsistently policed. Reconciling inconsistent status-quo-versus-alternative assumptions adds review time. rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of capital authorization delay caused by unresolved financial inputs
rows.imp_rat: Authorization delay from unresolved financial inputs is a process delay; capped at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financial loss caused by sterile bulk-solution contamination while awaiting fill-finish
rows.imp_rat: Financial loss from contamination during hold relates to the CFJ but ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic controls reduce frequency but the loss remains a severe, imperfectly-prevented outcome. Contamination losses during holding are realized suddenly and disrupt schedules. Preventing and valuing contamination loss requires sterility and cost-of-quality expertise. Financial loss from contaminated sterile bulk while awaiting fill-finish is a direct high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which committed capital exceeds the authorized vessel budget
rows.imp_rat: Committed capital exceeding the authorized budget is a financial-overrun error, remote from containment with budgetary consequence.

Controlling committed capital to the authorized budget requires disciplined change control the executor partly owns. Committed capital exceeding the authorized vessel budget is a direct and common overspend problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between planned and actual vessel availability for aseptic production
rows.imp_rat: Delay in vessel availability affects production timing but ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery-to-availability timing is uncertain and often slips. Delay between planned and actual vessel availability directly postpones aseptic production. Compressing availability delay requires project-execution expertise. Availability delay carries schedule and idle-capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of committed capital exposed to supplier nonperformance before vessel acceptance

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product, production, or regulatory changes make the investment unsuitable before planned payback
rows.imp_rat: Investment becoming unsuitable before payback is instability; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Long-horizon suitability is inherently uncertain and weakly managed today. Product or regulatory change before payback wastes the intended service period. Forecasting product and regulatory shifts over payback requires specialized foresight. An unsuitable investment before payback destroys expected value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of capital authorization revisions caused by post-approval changes to aseptic holding requirements

Estimating utility connection and disruption costs requires facilities engineering knowledge the executor must source externally. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of commissioning, qualification, validation, and regulatory documentation cost understated

Documentation cost is typically estimated near approval, adding late revisions when understated. Forecasting CQV and regulatory documentation effort requires regulatory-affairs experience rarely held by the purchaser. Validation and regulatory documentation costs are consistently understated because their effort is hard to predict up front.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning, sterilization, utility, maintenance, consumable, and spare-parts cost understated

Cleaning, sterilization and consumable costs are usually appended late, delaying a complete cost view. Estimating utility, maintenance and spare-parts spend requires operations expertise the executor lacks directly. Recurring cleaning, sterilization, utility and consumable costs are frequently understated in capital models.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among scope, warranty, service, and ownership assumptions used to compare acquisition options

rows.sat_rat: Acquisition-option comparisons are inconsistently normalized in status-quo evaluation. Normalizing scope and warranty assumptions across bids takes extra evaluation time. Comparing warranty, service and ownership terms consistently needs procurement expertise. Inconsistent ownership assumptions can hide true cost differences between options.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel service-life, replacement, and end-of-life assumptions prove inaccurate

rows.sat_rat: Service-life projections are commonly optimistic and inconsistently validated. Wrong service-life assumptions surface late, forcing unplanned timing changes. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of lifecycle expenditure counted more than once across acquisition, facility, compliance, and operating estimates
